items.headline: India launches its first private orbital rocket
items.title: India launches first private rocket into space - DW.com
items.scifi_hook: A privately built Indian rocket blasts off, marking the country's commercial space debut.
items.eli5: India's first non‑government rocket reached space, showing private companies can launch satellites.
items.why: It opens a new commercial market and reduces reliance on foreign launch services.
items.text: India launches first private rocket into space DW.com

items.headline: India achieves milestone with first private‑sector orbital launch
items.title: India achieves milestone with launch of first private-sector orbital rocket - Al Jazeera
items.scifi_hook: A home‑grown private rocket carries a payload into orbit, a first for India.
items.eli5: A privately funded Indian rocket successfully placed a satellite in space.
items.why: It signals a shift toward a domestic commercial launch industry.
items.text: India achieves milestone with launch of first private-sector orbital rocket Al Jazeera

items.headline: First private orbital launch lifts off from Sriharikota
items.title: First private orbital launch lifts off from Sriharikota - ISRO
items.scifi_hook: From Sriharikota, India's inaugural private rocket ignites, joining the global launch arena.
items.eli5: A privately built rocket launched from India's spaceport and reached orbit.
items.why: It diversifies launch options and could lower costs for satellite owners.
items.text: First private orbital launch lifts off from Sriharikota ISRO

items.headline: Agnico Eagle’s LaRonde mine uses world‑first automated rod magazine
items.title: Agnico Eagle Mines’ LaRonde operation becomes first to use Epiroc Diamec Automated Rod Magazine
items.scifi_hook: Robotic arms now feed drill rods deep underground, keeping workers out of danger.
items.eli5: A new machine automatically moves drilling rods in a Canadian gold mine, reducing manual work.
items.why: It improves safety and productivity in underground mining operations.
items.text: Located west of Val-d’Or, Quebec, the LaRonde mine, owned by Agnico Eagle Mines, has become the first underground mine in the world to use Epiroc’s new Diamec Automated Rod Magazine (ARM), first showcased at PDAC in Toronto earlier this year. Introduced and operated in the underground environment by drilling contractor Machines Roger International (MRI) crews, the ARM is an automated rod handling system designed to position operators further away from risk zones while improving operational efficiency. The system automates rod handling functions, including rods, swivel rods and inner tube handling, while reducing manual intervention and operator exposure to repetitive and potentially hazardous tasks, according to Epiroc. The project was carried out through close collaboration between Agnico Eagle’s LaRonde geology and operations teams, MRI’s underground drilling crews operating the equipment on site, and Epiroc’s technical and automation teams who completed this world-first commissioning. “We’re excited to partner with MRI on this project,” Michelle White, Business Line Manager Ore Body Solutions at Epiroc, says. “This collaboration showcases the benefits of utilising new technology to safely enhance the drilling experience underground.” The post Agnico Eagle Mines’ LaRonde operation becomes first to use Epiroc Diamec Automated Rod Magazine appeared first on International Mining .

items.headline: Pentagon awards $7.1 M to boost U.S. satellite cover‑glass production
items.title: Pentagon awards $7.1 million to expand U.S. production of cover glass for satellites
items.scifi_hook: A small U.S. firm will mass‑produce radiation‑proof glass for next‑gen spacecraft.
items.eli5: The U.S. government gave money to a company to make special glass for satellite parts.
items.why: It strengthens domestic supply chains for critical space hardware.
items.text: Defense Production Act funding was awarded to Martin Materials Solutions, a small business that makes radiation-resistant cover glass used on spacecraft solar arrays and thermal-control systems The post Pentagon awards $7.1 million to expand U.S. production of cover glass for satellites appeared first on SpaceNews .

items.headline: Pocket‑size AI phones showcased at China show
items.title: Pocket-size AI: Powerful phones star at China show
items.scifi_hook: Smartphones with built‑in AI agents can obey voice commands like personal assistants on steroids.
items.eli5: New phones in China can run AI helpers that understand speech and perform tasks for you.
items.why: They could drive a surge in AI‑driven hardware demand worldwide.
items.text: Tech firms are developing AI agent smartphones that could revolutionize mobile use. These advanced phones aim to perform tasks using voice commands and AI. Major app companies are restricting access, which hinders the AI agent's functionality. Companies like Nubia, Honor, and StepFun are showcasing these new devices. Experts predict a fundamental change in digital economies and business models.

items.headline: U.S. hits solar power milestone as fossil fuel use falls
items.title: U.S. reaches solar power milestone amid fossil fuel decline - Kiowa County Press
items.scifi_hook: Solar farms now generate more electricity than ever, edging out coal and gas.
items.eli5: The United States has added enough solar capacity to set a new record while using less fossil fuel.
items.why: It marks a tangible shift toward renewable electricity generation.
items.text: U.S. reaches solar power milestone amid fossil fuel decline Kiowa County Press
